name: Thylacine Sightings
description: Thylacine sightings refer to reported observations of the Thylacine (Thylacinus cynocephalus), also known as the Tasmanian Tiger, in mainland Australia and Tasmania after its official extinction in 1936. Proponents of ongoing Thylacine existence cite anecdotal evidence, including eyewitness accounts, blurry photographs and videos, and purported footprints or scat. These sightings are most frequently reported in remote areas of Tasmania, Western Australia, and Queensland. The persistence of such reports fuels the belief that a population of Thylacines may have survived undetected. Mainstream science generally dismisses these claims, citing a lack of conclusive physical evidence such as clear photographs, DNA samples, or carcasses. Skeptics argue that misidentification of other animals (dogs, foxes, feral cats) or deliberate hoaxes are more likely explanations. The dense vegetation and remote terrain of the Australian bush make definitive confirmation challenging, but the absence of tangible proof after decades of searching is considered strong evidence against the Thylacine's survival. Despite the lack of scientific validation, the Thylacine remains a powerful symbol of loss and a source of fascination in Australian folklore, driving continued interest in reported sightings and potential rediscovery efforts. The emotional investment in the Thylacine's survival often overshadows the critical evaluation of evidence.
subtitle: Claims of Thylacine survival after extinction.

claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts from credible individuals, including park rangers and farmers.
claims.criticism: Eyewitness accounts are subjective and prone to misidentification, especially under poor lighting or in unfamiliar environments. Confirmation bias plays a role.

claims.claim: Unclear photographs and videos depict Thylacines.
claims.evidence: Numerous blurry images and videos circulating online and in documentaries are purported to show Thylacines.
claims.criticism: The poor quality of these images and videos makes definitive identification impossible. They could easily be misidentified animals or digitally altered.

claims.claim: Footprints and scat found match Thylacine characteristics.
claims.evidence: Reports of footprints and scat that are larger than those of foxes or dogs and have unique features associated with the Thylacine.
claims.criticism: Footprint identification is notoriously unreliable, and scat analysis requires DNA testing, which has rarely been conducted successfully on purported Thylacine scat.

keyFigures.name: Col Bailey
keyFigures.role: Author and researcher who has documented numerous Thylacine sightings.

keyFigures.name: Nick Mooney
keyFigures.role: Zoologist and former Parks and Wildlife Service officer who has investigated many reported sightings and remains skeptical.

mythologyConnections: Aboriginal Dreamtime stories of similar creatures
European folklore of werewolves and shapeshifters (distant parallel in perceived ferocity)

timeline.event: Last known Thylacine dies in captivity at Hobart Zoo.
Intensive searches conducted after reported sightings in Tasmania.
Ongoing reports of sightings and sporadic search efforts across Australia.

sources.author: Paddle, R.
